config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. Society’s interests matter, but they should not automatically come before individual rights, because rights are what protect people from abuse by the majority or by the state. In my view, a society is strongest when it can solve shared problems without treating the individual as disposable. If the balance is wrong, “the public good” becomes an excuse to silence or exploit weaker people.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should automatically take priority over the individual, because in the United States that idea has too often been used to justify trampling minority rights and personal freedom. The whole point of rights is to put limits on what the majority, the government, or institutions can do, even when they claim it is for the common good. That said, individual rights are not a license to harm everyone else, so public safety and basic social order do matter. The right balance is not “society first,” but a system where the common good is pursued without making the individual disposable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From my perspective in Indonesia, I have seen how easily “the common good” can be used to pressure people into silence, conformity, or unfair treatment, especially when power is concentrated in the hands of officials or majorities. At the same time, individual rights should not be treated as an excuse to ignore public responsibilities, because a society also has to protect health, safety, and basic social trust. The real standard should be that society can ask for sacrifice only when it is necessary, fair, and limited, not whenever it is convenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual, because once you make that the default, it becomes too easy to excuse abuse in the name of the majority. From a United States perspective, our history shows how often “the common good” has been used to justify discrimination, censorship, and government overreach, which is exactly why individual rights exist as a barrier. I do not think rights are absolute in every situation, since public safety and real emergencies can require limits, but those limits have to be narrow and justified, not a blank check for society to override people. A healthy society protects the common good by respecting individuals, not by treating them as expendable.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not automatically override individual rights, and I say that quite strongly from an Indonesian perspective. In Indonesia, I have seen how appeals to harmony, order, or the public good can be used to pressure people into staying quiet, especially when the weaker side has little power to push back. Society does need the right to set limits in real emergencies and for public safety, but those limits must be necessary, fair, and temporary, not a convenient way to silence dissent or sacrifice minorities. To me, a society that respects individuals is stronger than one that demands obedience in the name of the collective.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that idea has too often been used to excuse coercion, discrimination, and government overreach. I do not buy the argument that the common good is enough on its own; history shows that majorities can be wrong, and rights are what protect people when popular opinion turns ugly. At the same time, I am not saying individual freedom means doing whatever you want, since real public safety and emergencies can justify limited restrictions. But those limits should be narrow, temporary, and accountable, not a standing excuse to make the individual secondary.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
